questions.question: Explain the steps involved in Heapsort to sort an array in ascending order using a max‑heap.
keyPoints: Build a max‑heap from the input array (heapify from the last non‑leaf upward).
Swap the root (maximum element) with the last unsorted element.
Reduce the heap size and apply max‑heapify to the root.
Repeat until the heap size becomes one; the array is then sorted.

questions.question: Describe the Counting Sort algorithm, including its assumptions, data structures used, and how the sorted output is produced.
keyPoints: Assumes input elements are non‑negative integers within range 0..k.
Create a count array of size k+1 and count occurrences of each element.
Transform the count array into cumulative counts to determine positions.
Traverse the input in reverse (to maintain stability) and place elements into the output array.

questions.question: Provide a detailed description of the Radix Sort algorithm (least‑significant‑digit version). Analyse its time and space complexity, discuss its stability, and indicate for which types of data it is most suitable.
keyPoints: Sorts numbers digit‑by‑digit starting from the least significant digit using a stable subroutine (e.g., Counting Sort).
Time complexity O(d·(n+k)) where d is the number of digits and k is the base (usually 10).
Space complexity O(n+k) due to the auxiliary counting sort; it is a stable sort.
Best suited for integers, fixed‑length strings, or any data that can be represented as a tuple of digits.

questions.question: Compare Counting Sort, Radix Sort, and Bucket Sort in terms of assumptions about input, time complexity, space complexity, stability, and typical use‑cases.
keyPoints: Counting Sort: works for non‑negative integers with bounded range, O(n+k) time, O(n+k) space, stable, ideal for small integer ranges.
Radix Sort: sorts digit‑by‑digit using a stable sub‑sort, O(d·(n+k)) time, O(n+k) space, stable, suitable for large integers or fixed‑length strings.
Bucket Sort: distributes elements into buckets based on a hash function, average O(n) time, O(n+k) space, not stable in general, works best for uniformly distributed data in a known interval.
Overall: linear time only under specific assumptions; each algorithm trades off extra space and input constraints.
